premises.content: In Figure 4, both the forward-only and backward-only variants are competitive, but the full Agentic Verifier (forward + backward) achieves the best accuracy on MATH500 and Gaokao2023 across N in {32, 64, 128} and across refinement rounds.
premises.label: ablation_observation

premises.content: The backward agent performs necessity checking by validating that the solution is grounded in the original problem constraints.
premises.label: backward_necessity_claim

premises.content: Single-turn GenRMs suffer from error propagation: because LLMs are mostly trained on correct or near-correct solutions, they struggle to issue a correct verdict when conditioned on a flawed solution and are easily misled by superficially plausible but incorrect answers.
premises.label: error_propagation_claim

premises.content: Single-turn GenRMs lack external grounding: without integration with symbolic solvers or external tools, they hallucinate on computation-intensive or knowledge-heavy tasks and exhibit unstable performance.
premises.label: external_grounding_gap_claim

premises.content: The forward agent performs sufficiency checking by validating that each preceding step is a sufficient condition for the subsequent derivation.
premises.label: forward_sufficiency_claim

premises.content: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B beats all listed baselines on both LiveCodeBench (competitive code) and HotpotQA (multi-hop QA) in Table 4.
premises.label: generalization_observation

premises.content: Generative Reward Models (GenRMs) trained with next-token prediction typically employ single-turn reasoning to assess candidate solutions.
premises.label: genrm_single_turn_claim

premises.content: The reported Table 5 numbers are accurate descriptions of the measured cost.
premises.label: latency_axiom

premises.content: Agentic Verifier consumes ~3.3x more tokens, ~11x more rounds, and ~2.7x more wall clock time than the base verifier; forward-only and backward-only variants sit between the two extremes.
premises.label: latency_observation

premises.content: Existing tool-augmented and LLM-as-Judge reward models either fail to tightly integrate tool execution into the reasoning process or do not provide point-wise feedback required for test-time scaling.
premises.label: prior_tool_augmented_rm_claim

premises.content: On Table 1, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves the best BoN accuracy in the Ours category across MATH500 (up to 79.0% at N=128), GSM8K (93.3%), Gaokao2023 (57.4%), and AIME24 (53.3%), surpassing the previous best ORM Skywork-V2-Llama-8B on MATH500 by 25.2 percentage points.
premises.label: table1_observation

premises.content: On Table 2, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B yields the highest accuracy and the largest Delta-up (e.g., 41.6% on MATH500, 40.3% on Gaokao2023 in turn 1) with low Delta-down, and remains stable across turns 2 and 3 while baselines such as DS-Distill-14B exhibit larger degradations.
premises.label: table2_observation

premises.content: Both agents can iteratively decompose complex solutions into verifiable sub-steps and invoke external tools (e.g., a Python interpreter) for numerical calculation.
premises.label: tool_augmented_capability_claim

premises.content: The efficacy of Test-Time Scaling for LLM reasoning is fundamentally dependent on the reward model (verifier) used to score and select candidate solutions.
premises.label: verifier_role_claim
